schools.summary: Ellicott counts the twenty years (7 for the temple, 13 for the palace, from the fourth year) and Keil maps the chapter's four movements, parallel to 1 Kings 9:10-28.
Clarke divides the span: seven and a half years building the temple, twelve and a half or thirteen his own house.
Henry paints the industrious king who spent his days in action, building and fortifying cities, in peace preparing for war.
Gill reads the notice as the point from which the Chronicler resumes the record of Solomon's works.

Ellicott and Keil harmonize with 1 Kings 9:11-13 via Josephus (Huram returned the despised 'land of Cabul' cities, which Solomon rebuilt and colonised), and defend the account as equally true, not a travesty of the older text.
Gill gives the same solution in brief: Solomon gave the cities, Huram disliked and returned them, Solomon built them up.
Clarke defers to his note on the transaction at 1 Kings 9:11.

debates.question: How does 'the cities Huram restored to Solomon' square with 1 Kings 9:11-13, where Solomon gives Huram cities?

sides.position: Huram returned the despised Galilean cities (the 'land of Cabul'), which Solomon then rebuilt and peopled with Israelites (Josephus, followed by the AV)
an exchange of friendly gifts, each king giving the other twenty cities

schools.summary: Keil notes the conquest is omitted in 1 Kings 9 and glosses 'prevailed against'; Ellicott, following Thenius, reads not a conquest of Hamath itself but the annexation of part of the Coele-Syrian plain.
Gill marks this as Solomon's one warlike expedition, a reduction of a rebel Coele-Syria subdued under David.

debates.question: Did Solomon conquer Hamath itself, or only annex part of its territory?

sides.position: he reduced a rebel Coele-Syria — his single warlike expedition
he annexed only part of Hamath's territory, the fruitful Coele-Syrian plain

schools.summary: Ellicott identifies Tadmor as Palmyra on the Euphrates caravan route and reads the Hamath store cities as outposts and victualling stations against Zobah-Damascus; Keil sets them in the ring of strongholds securing the kingdom.
Clarke names Tadmor as Palmyra, 'that superb city,' referring to his fuller account at 1 Kings 9:18.
Gill notes only, and briefly, the building of Tadmor in the wilderness.

Ellicott notes the Chronicler adds to 1 Kings 9:17 the fortification detail (walls, doors, bar) wanting in Kings, and reads 'built' as fortified; Keil classes both Beth-horons among the strongholds defending the kingdom.
Gill observes that of the pair it is the nether Beth-horon that is named elsewhere, the upper added here.

themes: fenced cities with walls, gates, bars

schools.summary: Ellicott places Baalath (a Danite bulwark against the Philistines) and notes the Chronicler's twice-added 'all' and his omission of Jerusalem, Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer; Keil carries the list into the Canaanite statute-labour of 1 Kings 9:20-23.
Gill notes the account now runs the same as the older record.
Clarke refers the store cities to his note at 1 Kings 9:19.
Henry draws the lesson: Solomon set bounds to his desires, finishing all he wished to build and then desiring no more.

themes: all that Solomon desired to build

schools.summary: Keil identifies the tributary levy as the surviving Canaanite remnant (Hittites, Amorites, and the rest), reads 'of their children' as non-partitive, defends the 'of' against 1 Kings 9:20-21, and ties the notice to the workmen-muster of 2 Chronicles 2:17.

themes: on whose shoulders the glory rose

schools.summary: Keil dwells on Israel's honours — men of war, princes, and 'adjutants' (not chariot-combatants, cf. Exodus 14:7; 2 Samuel 23:8), reading the words with 1 Kings 9:22.
Clarke marks that Solomon made no bondmen of Israel, referring to 1 Kings 9:21.

themes: the dignity of the covenant nation

schools.summary: Keil reads the 250 as the Israelite head-overseers over the Canaanite levy, reconciling them with the 550 of 1 Kings 9:23 (which counts Israelite and Canaanite chiefs together, cf. 2 Chronicles 2:17), and prefers the written 'praefecti' reading.

themes: the ordered hierarchy of the works

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the motive 'for he said' is the Chronicler's own addition (wanting in 1 Kings 9:24), 'king of Israel' set against the queen's Egyptian origin, and rejects Thenius; Keil grounds the removal in the ark's hallowing of David's palace.
Clarke reads the holiness-scruple as proof Solomon considered his wife a heathen, yet had not departed from the true God; Wesley: the house was kept pure from the very appearance of pollution.
Henry makes it a lesson on the ancient landmarks between things sacred and common, and on honouring David's memory.

Ellicott and Keil read 'offered' as habitual, note the altar 'before the porch' (not in 1 Kings 9:25), and see the divided worship of Gibeon (2 Chronicles 1:3) now healed as sacrifice comes home to the temple.
Gill guards 'then': not a one-time act but Solomon's constant practice.
Henry: the temple was built for its service; in vain the altar and the fire without the sacrifices constantly brought.

themes: the house built for its service

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'a day's matter on a day' (Leviticus 23:37) and notes the hapax form for 'solemn feasts'; Keil parses the daily-rate clause as an interpolated aside and names the three pilgrim feasts.
Gill fixes the ground-note: the daily morning-and-evening sacrifice of the law.
Henry gathers the daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly offerings into a pattern of settled devotion for the believer to imitate.

Ellicott glosses the offices (courses of 1 Chronicles 24, Levites' charges, 'to praise' of 1 Chronicles 25:3, ministry before the priests, porters of 1 Chronicles 26); Keil confirms Solomon kept David's whole arrangement unaltered.
Wesley explains 'the man of God': David as a prophet inspired in these matters, whose commands are God's commands.
Henry honours Solomon for adding nothing to the man of God's ordinance but enforcing it.
Gill names the four-and-twenty priestly courses serving in weekly turns.

Ellicott identifies 'the king' as David, restores the dropped preposition 'from,' and ties the treasures to 1 Chronicles 26:20-28; Keil reads the verb as 'transgress a command,' referring it to David's charge.
Clarke reads 'the commandment of the king' as the institutions of David.
Gill reads it of Solomon's commandment, which followed David's order and was in all things obeyed.

debates.question: Whose 'commandment of the king' did the priests not depart from?

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'prepared' as 'completed' (a late usage, cf. 2 Chronicles 29:35; 35:16), weighs the two-period division against the versions' 'from the day of the foundation,' and ties the close to 1 Kings 9:25; Keil fixes 'the day' as that on which the regular worship began.
Henry: the house was perfected only when its service was ordered — the work was the main matter, not the place.
Wesley: all the materials were procured and fitted beforehand.
Gill takes 'prepared' of the materials made ready, so nothing retarded the completion.

Ellicott dates the voyage after the temple, compares 1 Kings 9:26 ('a fleet at Ezion-geber near Eloth'), and cautions that the words need not imply a personal visit — Solomon may have sent his shipwrights.
Henry judges Solomon went in person to Eloth and Ezion-geber, for a man who deals in the world inspects his affairs himself.
Gill: Solomon, at leisure from building, turned to look after his navy and merchandise.
Clarke refers to his conjectures on Ezion-geber and Ophir at 1 Kings 9:26-28.

debates.question: Did Solomon go in person to the Edomite ports?

sides.position: yes, he visited Eloth and Ezion-geber himself to oversee the fleet
not necessarily; he sent his shipwrights to the port

schools.summary: Keil takes Ophir to be in southern Arabia; Ellicott works out the route (Tyrian ships to Joppa, crews overland to Ezion-geber) and lays the 450-vs-420-talent difference from 1 Kings 9:28 to a scribe's confusion of Hebrew numerals.
Clarke explains the need for Tyrian sailors — Solomon bore the cost, Huram the seamen, since the Jews had no skill in maritime affairs.

themes: how the ships reached the Red Sea
